era_context: The mid-15th century marks a pivotal moment in world history: the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in the Balkans, European states are engaged in the final phases of the Hundred Years' War, the Italian Renaissance is flourishing, the Ming Dynasty is at its height in China, Portuguese maritime exploration is accelerating, and the Inca Empire is expanding in South America. This is an era of transition from medieval to early modern structures across multiple civilisations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan African kingdoms (limited European documentation)
Central Asian and Steppe polities (sparse written records in European sources)
Pacific Islander societies (no contemporary written records)
Southeast Asian kingdoms (limited documentation in European sources)

events.title: Gutenberg begins printing press development
events.description: Johannes Gutenberg is developing the movable-type printing press in Mainz. By 1440, he is likely working on the key innovations that will lead to the printing press, including the use of metal type, oil-based ink, and the printing mechanism. The exact timeline of his innovations remains debated, but by the mid-1440s, he had likely perfected the technology. This innovation will revolutionise information dissemination across Europe, though the first dated printed work (the 42-line Bible) would not appear until circa 1455.

sources.name: Gutenberg's legal documents and contracts (Mainz archives)
Later accounts by Johann Trithemius and others
Historiographic consensus from printing history scholarship

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Gutenberg's innovations is uncertain. Documents place him in Mainz by 1444, but the development of the press likely occurred over several years (1440–1455). The 1440 date is conventional; the technology was probably not fully mature until the early 1450s.
cross_references: Led to 1455-001 (Printing of the Gutenberg Bible)

events.title: Eton College founded
events.description: King Henry VI of England founds Eton College, a prestigious school near Windsor. The college is established as a centre of learning and is intended to educate boys for service to the Church and the Crown. Eton College becomes one of the most prestigious educational institutions in England and remains influential in English education to the present day. The founding reflects the growing emphasis on education and humanist learning in the 15th century.

sources.name: Royal charter and founding documents
English royal chronicles
Eton College records

events.certainty_note: The founding of Eton College is well-documented in royal charters and college records. The date of 1440 is firmly established.
cross_references: Part of broader educational expansion in 15th-century England

events.title: King's College, Cambridge founded
events.description: King Henry VI of England founds King's College, Cambridge, as part of his broader educational patronage. The college is established to educate boys from Eton College and to provide a centre of learning at the University of Cambridge. King's College becomes one of the most prestigious colleges at Cambridge and remains influential in English education. The founding reflects the growing emphasis on education and humanist learning in the 15th century.

sources.name: Royal charter and founding documents
English royal chronicles
Cambridge University records

events.certainty_note: The founding of King's College, Cambridge is well-documented in royal charters and university records. The date of 1440 is firmly established.
cross_references: Part of broader educational expansion in 15th-century England

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continue systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1440, Portuguese caravels have reached the Senegal River and are establishing trading posts and forts. These expeditions are motivated by the search for gold, spices, and a sea route to Asia, as well as the conversion of African peoples to Christianity. The voyages mark the beginning of European colonial expansion in Africa.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and records
Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea
Venetian merchant reports

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration is well-documented in royal records and contemporary chronicles. The specific achievements of 1440 are attested in Zurara's chronicle, though written after the events.
cross_references: Part of broader Age of Exploration (1415 onwards)

events.title: Inca expansion under Pachacuti
events.description: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui, the ninth Inca emperor, continues his military campaigns to expand the Inca Empire. By 1440, he has already defeated the Chancas and is consolidating control over the Andean region. His reign marks the beginning of the Inca Empire's rapid expansion, which will eventually create the largest pre-Columbian state in the Americas. Pachacuti's military innovations and administrative reforms transform the Inca state into a centralised empire.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca oral tradition recorded by Spanish chroniclers)
Pedro Cieza de León, Chronicle of Peru
Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala, Nueva Corónica y Buen Gobierno

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is notoriously uncertain, as the Inca did not use a written calendar system. The date of 1440 is assigned by modern scholars based on Spanish colonial sources and oral traditions recorded centuries after the events. The actual date could vary by several decades.
cross_references: Led to 1532-001 (Spanish conquest of the Inca Empire)

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidation
events.description: The Ming Dynasty continues to consolidate its power and expand its influence in East Asia. By 1440, the Ming state has established a sophisticated bureaucratic system based on Confucian principles and the examination system. The reign of the Xuande Emperor (1426–1435) and his successors has seen the stabilisation of Ming rule and the expansion of Chinese influence. The Ming state is at the height of its power and cultural achievement.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Ming administrative records

events.certainty_note: Ming administrative structures and consolidation are well-documented in Ming records. The state of the Ming Dynasty in 1440 is attested in multiple sources.

disconfirming_evidence: Gutenberg's invention of the printing press is often attributed solely to him, but woodblock printing and earlier movable-type systems existed in Asia, and some scholars argue that Gutenberg's specific innovation was the combination of technologies rather than the invention of printing itself. The founding of Eton College and King's College, Cambridge are sometimes presented as purely educational initiatives, but they were also instruments of royal power and control. Portuguese exploration is often presented as driven by noble ideals of discovery and conversion, but the primary motives were economic (gold, spices) and the beginning of the slave trade. Pachacuti's dates are highly uncertain; some scholars place his reign earlier or later, and the extent of his conquests by 1440 is debated. The Ming Dynasty's consolidation is sometimes presented as a period of stability, but internal conflicts and the beginning of the shift toward isolationism were already underway.
historiographic_note: The year 1440 is well-documented in European sources (English, Portuguese, papal records) but less thoroughly in Asian sources available to Western scholarship. The founding of Eton College and King's College, Cambridge are extensively documented in royal charters and institutional records. Portuguese exploration is documented in royal records and contemporary chronicles, but African perspectives are almost entirely absent from contemporary written sources. Chinese records are rich but often filtered through later compilations. The historiography of this period is heavily shaped by European state records; the experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups are largely invisible. Inca chronology is particularly problematic, as the Inca did not use a written calendar system, and Spanish colonial sources are often contradictory.

graph_edges.note: Gutenberg's development of the printing press in 1440 led to the printing of the Gutenberg Bible circa 1455.
The founding of Eton College and King's College, Cambridge were part of the same educational patronage initiative by Henry VI.
Portuguese exploration of the African coast in 1440 led to the beginning of the Portuguese slave trade in 1441.
Pachacuti's expansion of the Inca Empire in 1440 created the state that would be conquered by Spanish conquistadors in 1532.
Ming consolidation in 1440 was disrupted by the Tumu Crisis in 1449.

era_context: The mid-15th century was marked by the final stages of the Hundred Years' War in Western Europe, the consolidation of the Ming Dynasty in China under the Yongle Emperor's successors, the Ottoman Empire's expansion in the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans, and the early Portuguese Age of Exploration along the African coast. In Europe, the Council of Constance's aftermath shaped religious reform movements, while in East Asia, the Zheng He voyages had recently concluded and maritime exploration was being curtailed.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; Portuguese sources begin to appear)
Interior Americas (no European documentation; Aztec and Inca records exist but are sparse for this specific year)
Central Asia and Steppe regions (limited contemporary sources)

events.title: Battle of Herrings (Journée des Harengs)
events.description: English forces under Sir John Fastolf defeated a French and Scottish relief column attempting to supply the besieged town of Orléans during the Hundred Years' War. The battle is remembered for the English use of defensive tactics and the French failure to break the English supply lines. This engagement demonstrated the continued effectiveness of English archers and disciplined formations, though it was a minor engagement overshadowed by larger campaigns. The victory temporarily secured English supply routes in Normandy.

sources.name: Jean de Wavrin, Recueil des croniques et anchiennes istories de la Grant Bretaigne
Enguerrand de Monstrelet, Chronicle

events.certainty_note: Date and basic facts confirmed by multiple near-contemporary chronicles, though written decades after the event. The battle occurred on 12 February 1429 in some sources, but 1439 dating appears in certain compilations; this requires verification.
cross_references: Relates to broader Hundred Years' War context (1337–1453)

events.title: Council of Florence (Ecumenical Council)
events.description: The Council of Florence, which began in 1431, continued its work toward reconciliation between the Roman Catholic Church and the Eastern Orthodox Church. In 1439, the council achieved a temporary union agreement (Decree of Union) between East and West, signed on 6 July. The agreement addressed the filioque controversy, the use of leavened vs. unleavened bread in the Eucharist, and papal primacy. However, the union proved short-lived and was repudiated by many Orthodox bishops upon their return to Constantinople, particularly after the Ottoman conquest in 1453.
key_figures: Cardinal Bessarion (Orthodox delegate, later Catholic cardinal)

sources.name: Acta Concilii Florentini (Official council records)
Vespasiano da Bisticci, Lives of Illustrious Men

events.certainty_note: The council's proceedings are well-documented in official records and contemporary accounts. The date of the union decree (6 July 1439) is firmly established.
cross_references: Part of broader religious reform movements of the 15th century

events.title: Portuguese Exploration: Cape Bojador Expeditions
events.region: West African coast (Cape Bojador region, modern Western Sahara)
events.description: Portuguese explorers, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continued systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1439, Portuguese caravels had made multiple attempts to round Cape Bojador, a psychological and navigational barrier. These expeditions gathered information about African geography, trade routes, and indigenous peoples. The voyages were motivated by desires for gold, spices, and knowledge of African kingdoms, and they marked the beginning of sustained European maritime exploration of Africa.
key_figures: Diogo Gomes (Portuguese explorer, credited with rounding Cape Bojador)

sources.name: Gomes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea
Diogo Gomes, De prima inventione Guinee

events.certainty_note: The exact year of Cape Bojador's first successful rounding is disputed in sources. Some sources place it in 1434, others in 1436 or later. 1439 is assigned here by convention as a representative year of sustained exploration efforts.
cross_references: Part of Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1522)
Leads to establishment of trading posts and eventual transatlantic slave trade

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Zheng He's Final Voyages Conclude
events.description: The great treasure voyages of Admiral Zheng He, which had begun in 1405, were winding down by 1439. Zheng He himself died in 1433, but his final voyage (the seventh) returned to China around 1433. By 1439, the Ming court had largely ceased sponsoring large-scale maritime expeditions, redirecting resources to land-based military concerns and the Great Wall. This marked a turning point in global exploration: while Europe was beginning sustained oceanic exploration, China withdrew from it, a decision with profound long-term consequences.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming ruler, died 1424, but his policies continued)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records)
Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The conclusion of Zheng He's voyages and the shift in Ming policy are well-documented in official Ming records and contemporary accounts by voyage participants.
cross_references: Part of broader Ming Dynasty consolidation (1368–1644)

events.title: Ottoman Empire: Consolidation in the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad II continued its consolidation of power in the Balkans following the Ottoman-Venetian War (1426–1432). By 1439, Ottoman control extended over much of the former Serbian and Bulgarian territories. The Ottomans were establishing administrative systems and military garrisons throughout the region. This period saw the integration of Balkan territories into the Ottoman administrative structure, with the devshirme system (recruitment of Christian boys for military and administrative service) becoming more systematized.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and administrative documents
Ashikpashazade, Tevarih-i Al-i Osman (History of the House of Osman)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans during this period is well-attested, though specific events for 1439 are not always precisely dated in sources.
cross_references: Leads to Ottoman conquest of Constantinople (1453-001)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Herrings (1439-001) presents a dating discrepancy: some sources place this battle in 1429 rather than 1439. Modern scholarship generally accepts 1429 as the correct date, making the inclusion of this event under 1439 questionable. The Council of Florence's union decree is sometimes dated to 1438 in certain sources, though 1439 is the more widely accepted date. The conclusion of Zheng He's voyages is sometimes attributed to 1433 rather than 1439, though the broader policy shift away from maritime exploration continued through 1439.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1439 is moderate but unevenly distributed. European sources (particularly Italian, French, and Portuguese) are relatively abundant, while Ottoman sources are more limited in English-language scholarship. Chinese sources are rich but require specialized knowledge to access. African sources are minimal—the Portuguese perspective dominates the exploration narrative. The historical record is shaped by the survival of written chronicles and official records, which tend to emphasize political and military events over economic or social developments. There is a significant Western bias in available English-language sources.

graph_edges.note: The Council of Florence's union was repudiated after the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople, which the union had hoped to prevent
Portuguese exploration of Africa was part of the broader Age of Exploration
Chinese maritime exploration was concluding while European exploration was beginning
Ottoman consolidation in the Balkans was a precursor to the conquest of Constantinople

This was a period of significant religious, political, and exploratory transition across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records)
Interior Americas (no European documentation)
Central Asia and Steppe regions (limited contemporary sources)
Southeast Asia (limited European sources; local chronicles exist but are underrepresented)

events.title: Council of Florence: Continued Ecumenical Negotiations
events.description: The Council of Florence, which had begun in 1431, continued its work in 1438 toward reconciliation between the Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches. Delegates from Constantinople, including Emperor John VIII Palaiologus and Patriarch Joseph II, were present to negotiate union. The council addressed fundamental theological and liturgical differences. Though negotiations were ongoing, the groundwork for the 1439 union decree was being laid during this year.
key_figures: Patriarch Joseph II (Orthodox Patriarch of Constantinople)

sources.name: Acta Concilii Florentini (Official council records)
Sylvester Syropoulos, Memoirs of the Council of Florence

events.certainty_note: The council's proceedings are well-documented in official records and contemporary accounts by participants.
cross_references: Directly precedes the union decree of 1439-002
Part of broader religious reform movements

events.title: Pragmatic Sanction of Bourges
events.description: King Charles VII of France issued the Pragmatic Sanction of Bourges on 7 July 1438, which significantly limited papal authority in France and established the French church's independence from Rome. The sanction restricted papal taxation of the French clergy, limited papal appointments in France, and asserted royal control over ecclesiastical matters. This was a major assertion of royal power over the church and influenced the relationship between secular and religious authority throughout Western Europe. The sanction remained in effect (with interruptions) until 1516.

sources.name: The Pragmatic Sanction of Bourges (official text)
Jean Chartier, Chronicle of Charles VII

events.certainty_note: The date (7 July 1438) and content of the Pragmatic Sanction are well-documented in official records and contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: Reflects broader tension between secular and religious authority in the 15th century
Precedes similar assertions of royal authority over the church in other European kingdoms

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Xuande Emperor's Reign
events.description: The Xuande Emperor (r. 1426–1435) had recently died in 1435, and his successor, the Zhengtong Emperor, was a child. By 1438, the young emperor's reign was being managed by regents and eunuchs, particularly the powerful eunuch Wang Zhen. This period saw the continuation of Ming administrative consolidation and the gradual shift away from maritime exploration toward land-based military concerns. The court was focused on maintaining stability and managing the succession.
key_figures: Zhengtong Emperor (Ming ruler, r. 1435–1449, child during this period)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records)
Sima Guang, Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The succession and regency arrangements are well-documented in Ming official records.
cross_references: Leads to the Tumu Crisis of 1449

events.title: Inca Empire: Pachacuti's Expansion Continues
events.description: The Inca Empire under Pachacuti (r. 1438–1471) was in the early stages of its rapid territorial expansion. Pachacuti is traditionally dated to have begun his reign in 1438, though some sources place this date earlier. He initiated a series of military campaigns that would transform the Inca state from a regional power into a vast empire. By 1438, Pachacuti was consolidating control over the Cusco region and beginning campaigns against neighboring peoples. His reign marked a turning point in Inca history, characterized by administrative reforms, architectural achievements, and territorial conquest.
key_figures: Viracocha (previous Inca ruler, father of Pachacuti)

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record keepers' oral traditions, recorded later by Spanish chroniclers)
Pedro Cieza de León, Chronicle of Peru
Garcilaso de la Vega, Royal Commentaries of the Incas

events.certainty_note: The dating of Pachacuti's reign is based on Inca oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers decades or centuries after the events. The exact year of 1438 is traditional and may be off by several years. Archaeological evidence supports the general period but not the precise date.
cross_references: Leads to Inca conquest of much of South America by 1471

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English Losses in France
events.description: The Hundred Years' War continued with English forces gradually losing ground to French forces under the leadership of Joan of Arc's successors and French military reformers. By 1438, English control in France was contracting, particularly in Normandy and Aquitaine. The English were facing increasing pressure from French forces and were beginning to lose the military advantage they had held earlier in the century. This period saw the consolidation of French national identity and military strength.
key_figures: Henry VI (King of England, child during this period)

sources.name: Jean de Wavrin, Recueil des croniques et anchiennes istories de la Grant Bretaigne
Enguerrand de Monstrelet, Chronicle

events.certainty_note: The general trend of English losses during this period is well-attested, though specific events for 1438 are not always precisely dated.
cross_references: Leads to English loss of most French territories by 1453

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Pachacuti's reign (1438-004) is highly disputed. Some sources place his accession earlier (1430s) or later (1440s). The traditional date of 1438 is based on Inca oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers, which are inherently unreliable for precise dating. Archaeological evidence does not provide a definitive date for Pachacuti's reign. The Pragmatic Sanction of Bourges (1438-002) is sometimes dated to 1438 and sometimes to 1439 in different sources, though 1438 is more commonly accepted.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1438 is moderate but unevenly distributed. European sources are relatively abundant, particularly for religious and political events. Chinese sources are rich but require specialized knowledge. Inca sources are problematic—they rely on oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers decades or centuries after the events, introducing significant uncertainty. The historical record is shaped by the survival of written chronicles and official records, which tend to emphasize political and military events. There is a significant Western and European bias in available English-language sources, with non-European civilizations underrepresented.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Xuande Emperor's Death and Succession
events.description: The Xuande Emperor of the Ming Dynasty died in January 1435 (some sources date this to 1435, others to early 1436). His young son, the Zhengtong Emperor, succeeded him at age 8 or 9. By 1437, the Zhengtong Emperor's reign was being managed by regents and powerful eunuchs, particularly Wang Zhen. This succession marked a shift in Ming governance, with eunuchs gaining increasing influence over court affairs. The young emperor's regency period would last until 1449, when he was captured in the Tumu Crisis.
key_figures: Zhengtong Emperor (Ming ruler, r. 1435–1449, child during this period)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records)

events.certainty_note: The succession and dates are well-documented in Ming official records, though the exact date of the Xuande Emperor's death varies slightly between sources (1435 or early 1436).
cross_references: Leads to the Tumu Crisis of 1449

events.title: Council of Florence: Continued Negotiations
events.description: The Council of Florence continued its work in 1437 toward reconciliation between the Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches. Delegates from Constantinople were present, and negotiations were progressing toward a union agreement. The council was addressing fundamental theological differences, including the filioque controversy and the use of leavened vs. unleavened bread in the Eucharist. The negotiations would culminate in the union decree of 1439.

sources.name: Acta Concilii Florentini (Official council records)

events.certainty_note: The council's proceedings are well-documented in official records.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: Siege of Calais and English Losses
events.description: The Hundred Years' War continued with English forces facing increasing pressure from French forces. By 1437, English control in France was contracting, particularly in Normandy and Aquitaine. The English were losing the military advantage they had held earlier in the century. This period saw the consolidation of French national identity and military strength under Charles VII. The English would eventually lose Calais in 1558, but the process of English withdrawal from France was well underway by 1437.
key_figures: Henry VI (King of England, child during this period)

events.title: Hundred Years' War: Treaty of Arras
events.description: The Treaty of Arras was signed in September 1435, ending the alliance between England and Burgundy during the Hundred Years' War. The treaty was negotiated between France and Burgundy, with Burgundy agreeing to withdraw from its alliance with England. This was a major diplomatic victory for France, as it isolated England and strengthened France's position in the war. The treaty marked a turning point in the Hundred Years' War, as England's position in France would continue to deteriorate after this date.

era_context: The early 15th century marks the height of the Chinese Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor's successors, the consolidation of the Ottoman Empire in Anatolia and the Balkans, the Italian Renaissance in its early flowering, and the Hussite Wars' aftermath in Central Europe. The Council of Constance (1414–1418) has recently concluded; the Great Schism in the Catholic Church is healing. In Africa, the Portuguese are beginning systematic Atlantic exploration. In Mesoamerica, the Aztec Empire under Montezuma I is expanding.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records outside Ethiopia and coastal trading posts)
Interior Southeast Asia (Khmer, Lao, Thai kingdoms have sparse European documentation)
Oceania and Pacific islands
North American interior (no written records; archaeological dating imprecise)

events.title: Council of Constance concludes; Jan Hus's legacy and ecclesiastical reform
events.description: The Council of Constance, which began in 1414, formally concludes its sessions in 1434. The council had condemned Jan Hus (executed 1415) and Jerome of Prague (executed 1416) for heresy, but their theological legacy—particularly critiques of clerical corruption and calls for communion in both kinds—persists and influences the Hussite movement in Bohemia. The council's work on ecclesiastical reform and the healing of the Western Schism represents a major reassertion of papal authority, though implementation remains contested. The council's decisions shape Catholic reform agendas for the next century.

sources.name: Ulrich of Richenthal, Chronik des Konstanzer Konzils (contemporary chronicle, written during the council)
Acta Concilii Constanciensis (official council records)

events.certainty_note: The Council of Constance is one of the best-documented ecclesiastical events of the medieval period, with extensive contemporary records and multiple eyewitness accounts.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet returns to China; end of major maritime expeditions
events.description: Zheng He, the Muslim eunuch admiral of the Ming Dynasty, completes his seventh and final major voyage of the treasure fleet (Baochuan). The fleet, which had undertaken massive expeditions across the Indian Ocean since 1405, returns to Nanjing in 1434. This voyage reached East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian subcontinent. After 1434, the Ming court, under pressure from Confucian officials and facing fiscal constraints, ceases to fund large-scale maritime expeditions. The decision marks a turning point: China withdraws from oceanic exploration just as European maritime expansion accelerates. The treasure fleet's voyages represent the largest and most technologically advanced naval expeditions of the early 15th century, but their discontinuation has profound long-term consequences for global trade and geopolitical power.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (deceased 1424, but his policies continued under successors)
Xuande Emperor (reigning 1426–1435, patron of the final voyage)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records), official court records
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores), written by a member of Zheng He's expeditions
Fei Xin, Xingcha Shenglan (The Overall Survey of the Star Raft), another expedition participant's account

events.certainty_note: The dates and scale of Zheng He's voyages are well-documented in Ming court records and contemporary accounts by expedition participants. The 1434 return date is established in official records.

events.title: Cosimo de' Medici returns to Florence; consolidation of Medici power
events.description: Cosimo de' Medici, the patriarch of the Medici banking family, returns to Florence after a year of exile (1433–1434) imposed by the rival Albizzi family. His return marks a decisive shift in Florentine politics: the Medici faction, backed by popular support and their financial networks, consolidates power. Cosimo's return initiates the Medici's rise to de facto rule of Florence—a dominance that will last nearly three centuries and make Florence a center of Renaissance patronage, art, and humanist learning. The Medici will become the primary patrons of figures like Brunelleschi, Donatello, and later Michelangelo and Leonardo da Vinci.

sources.name: Giovanni Villani and continuators, Cronica (Florentine chronicle)
Vespasiano da Bisticci, Lives of Illustrious Men (later 15th-century biographical compilation)

events.certainty_note: Cosimo's exile and return are well-documented in Florentine chronicles and later Renaissance biographical sources. The date of his return in 1434 is established.

events.title: Portuguese exploration: Gil Eanes rounds Cape Bojador
events.description: Portuguese navigator Gil Eanes, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, successfully rounds Cape Bojador on the West African coast—a feat long considered impossible by European sailors due to treacherous currents, shoals, and psychological barriers (the cape was thought to mark the edge of the habitable world). Eanes's successful rounding in 1434 opens the African coast to systematic Portuguese exploration and trade. This voyage marks the beginning of the Age of Exploration from the European perspective and initiates the Portuguese slave trade, which will expand dramatically over the next century. The rounding of Cape Bojador is a watershed moment: it demonstrates that European ships can navigate previously impassable waters and encourages further expeditions southward along the African coast.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronica do Descobrimento e Conquista de Guiné (Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea), written mid-15th century
Portuguese royal records and navigational logs (fragmentary)

events.certainty_note: The rounding of Cape Bojador is traditionally dated to 1434, though some sources place it in 1433. The event is attested in later Portuguese chronicles but not in detailed contemporary documentation. The date is accepted by historiographic consensus but carries ±1 year uncertainty.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: Hussite Wars: Battle of Lipany; radical Taborites defeated
events.description: The Battle of Lipany (30 May 1434) marks a decisive turning point in the Hussite Wars. The radical Taborite faction, which had dominated Hussite military campaigns since the 1420s, is defeated by a coalition of moderate Hussites (Utraquists) and Catholic forces. The Taborites, who advocated radical social and religious reform, are crushed; their leader Procopius the Great is killed. The battle effectively ends the radical phase of the Hussite movement and leads to the Compactata of Iglau (1436), which grants limited concessions to moderate Hussites while reintegrating Bohemia into the Catholic fold. The defeat of the Taborites represents a broader pattern: radical religious and social movements of the early 15th century are being suppressed or moderated by established powers.
key_figures: Procopius the Great (Taborite leader, killed at Lipany)
John Žižka (deceased 1424, but his legacy shapes the battle)

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Hussite Chronicle (contemporary Hussite account)
Catholic chronicles and records of the period

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Lipany is well-documented in both Hussite and Catholic sources. The date (30 May 1434) and outcome are firmly established.

events.title: Aztec Empire: Montezuma I consolidates power; expansion campaigns
events.description: Montezuma I (Montezuma Ilhuicamina), who became Aztec emperor in 1440, is not yet in power in 1434, but the period 1434–1440 represents the reign of his predecessor Itzcóatl, during which the Aztec Triple Alliance consolidates control over the Valley of Mexico and begins aggressive expansion. Itzcóatl's reign (1427–1440) sees the Aztec defeat of the Azcapotzalco and the establishment of Aztec hegemony. By 1434, the Aztec are the dominant military power in central Mexico, and the stage is set for Montezuma I's reign (1440–1468), which will see the empire reach its territorial and cultural zenith. The period is marked by the construction of the Great Temple (Templo Mayor) and the codification of Aztec religious and administrative systems.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (Aztec pictorial manuscript, compiled post-conquest but based on pre-conquest sources)
Sahagún, Florentine Codex (Spanish friar's compilation of Aztec accounts, 16th century)
Archaeological evidence from the Templo Mayor excavations

events.certainty_note: Aztec chronology for the early 15th century is reconstructed from post-conquest sources and archaeological evidence. The Aztec calendar system (xiuhpohualli) is well-understood, but the correlation with the Gregorian calendar carries ±2–3 year uncertainty for this period. The reign of Itzcóatl is confirmed, but specific events within 1434 are not precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of Gil Eanes's rounding of Cape Bojador to 1434 is questioned by some modern scholars, who place the event in 1433 or suggest that earlier Portuguese expeditions may have reached the cape. The significance attributed to this voyage as the 'beginning of the Age of Exploration' is also contested: some historians argue that Portuguese exploration was more gradual and less revolutionary than the traditional narrative suggests. Additionally, the claim that Cape Bojador was previously considered impassable is partly legendary; earlier Portuguese and Genoese navigators had likely approached the cape, though Eanes's voyage may have been the first successful rounding with documented return. The Hussite Wars' significance is sometimes overstated in Western historiography; while important for Central European religious and political history, the wars had limited impact on global events compared to concurrent developments in China, the Ottoman Empire, or the Italian Renaissance.
historiographic_note: The year 1434 is well-documented for Europe (particularly Italy, the Holy Roman Empire, and Portugal) due to abundant chronicle sources, administrative records, and later Renaissance historiography. Documentation for Ming China is also rich, thanks to official court records (Ming Shilu). However, sources for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or entirely absent from European-language historiography. The Aztec chronology relies heavily on post-conquest compilations, which may contain errors or biases introduced by Spanish friars. Portuguese exploration narratives are shaped by later nationalist historiography and may exaggerate the significance of early voyages. The historical record for 1434 is heavily weighted toward Europe and East Asia; other regions are underrepresented.

Zheng He's final voyage is the culmination of the treasure fleet expeditions that began in 1405.
Cosimo de' Medici's return from exile in 1434 directly follows his exile in 1433 and initiates the Medici's rise to power.
Gil Eanes's rounding of Cape Bojador initiates the Age of Exploration, which culminates in Columbus's Atlantic voyage 58 years later.
The Battle of Lipany is a major engagement in the Hussite Wars, which began in 1419.
The consolidation of Aztec power under Itzcóatl (1427–1440) sets the stage for Montezuma I's reign and the empire's expansion.

events.title: Cosimo de' Medici exiled from Florence; Albizzi faction ascendant
events.description: Cosimo de' Medici, the powerful patriarch of the Medici banking family, is exiled from Florence by the rival Albizzi faction. The exile lasts approximately one year (1433–1434). Cosimo's exile represents a temporary setback for the Medici but ultimately strengthens their position: his absence generates popular sympathy, and his financial networks remain intact. The exile is a turning point in Florentine factional politics, demonstrating the fragility of power in the republic and foreshadowing the Medici's eventual dominance. Cosimo's return in 1434 will be triumphant and will initiate the Medici's rise to de facto rule of Florence.

sources.name: Giovanni Villani and continuators, Cronica (Florentine chronicle)
Vespasiano da Bisticci, Lives of Illustrious Men (later 15th-century biographical compilation)

events.certainty_note: Cosimo's exile in 1433 is well-documented in Florentine chronicles. The date and duration are firmly established.

events.title: Council of Constance: final sessions and ecclesiastical reform
events.description: The Council of Constance, which began in 1414, continues its final sessions in 1433. The council has already condemned Jan Hus (1415) and Jerome of Prague (1416) for heresy, and has worked to heal the Western Schism. In 1433, the council is addressing remaining ecclesiastical reform issues and preparing for its formal conclusion in 1434. The council's work represents a major reassertion of papal authority and an attempt to address corruption and schism within the Catholic Church. The council's decisions will shape Catholic reform agendas for the next century and influence the Protestant Reformation indirectly.

sources.name: Ulrich of Richenthal, Chronik des Konstanzer Konzils (contemporary chronicle)
Acta Concilii Constanciensis (official council records)

events.certainty_note: The Council of Constance's sessions are extensively documented. The 1433 date is confirmed in official records.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet: sixth major voyage underway
events.description: Zheng He's treasure fleet is in the midst of its sixth major voyage (1433–1435), which will be one of the largest and most ambitious expeditions. The fleet, comprising hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel, is exploring the Indian Ocean, reaching East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian subcontinent. The voyage demonstrates Ming China's technological superiority in naval construction and navigation. However, the expeditions are increasingly opposed by Confucian officials at court, who view them as wasteful and contrary to Confucian principles of frugality and inward focus. After this voyage concludes in 1434–1435, the Ming court will cease to fund large-scale maritime expeditions, marking a decisive shift in Chinese policy.
key_figures: Xuande Emperor (reigning 1426–1435, patron of the voyage)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records), official court records
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The dates and scale of Zheng He's voyages are well-documented in Ming court records. The sixth voyage is dated to 1433–1435 in official sources.

events.title: Hussite Wars: continued military campaigns in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars continue in 1433, with the radical Taborite faction conducting military campaigns against Catholic forces and moderate Hussite opponents. The Taborites, inspired by the legacy of Jan Hus and John Žižka, advocate radical social and religious reform. In 1433, the Taborites are at the height of their military power, though they face increasing opposition from a coalition of moderate Hussites (Utraquists) and Catholic forces. The year 1433 represents the final phase of radical Hussite dominance; the Battle of Lipany in 1434 will decisively defeat the Taborites and end the radical phase of the movement.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Hussite Chronicle (contemporary Hussite account)
Catholic chronicles and records

events.certainty_note: The Hussite Wars are well-documented, but specific military campaigns within 1433 are not always precisely dated. The general context of Taborite military activity is confirmed.

events.title: Portuguese exploration: early Atlantic voyages and African coast reconnaissance
events.description: Portuguese navigators, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, are conducting early expeditions along the West African coast in 1433. These voyages are preliminary to the more famous rounding of Cape Bojador in 1434. The Portuguese are systematically exploring the African coast, establishing trading posts, and gathering information about African geography and resources. The expeditions are motivated by commercial interests (gold, spices, slaves) and religious zeal (conversion of African peoples). The year 1433 represents the early phase of Portuguese Atlantic exploration, which will accelerate dramatically over the next decades.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)
Gil Eanes (navigator, who will round Cape Bojador in 1434)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronica do Descobrimento e Conquista de Guiné (later 15th-century chronicle)
Portuguese royal records (fragmentary)

events.certainty_note: Early Portuguese exploration is documented in later chronicles and fragmentary royal records. Specific events within 1433 are not precisely dated; the year is assigned by convention to represent the period of early exploration.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Portuguese exploration as a continuous, systematic effort beginning in the 1430s is questioned by some scholars, who argue that exploration was more sporadic and less coordinated than later Portuguese historiography suggests. The significance of Prince Henry the Navigator's role is also debated; some historians argue that his influence has been exaggerated by later sources. Additionally, the claim that Portuguese exploration was driven primarily by religious zeal (conversion) is complicated by the fact that the Portuguese slave trade, which began in the 1440s, became the primary economic motivation. The Hussite Wars' significance in European history is sometimes overstated; while important for Central European religious and political development, the wars had limited impact on global events.
historiographic_note: The year 1433 is well-documented for Europe, particularly Italy and Central Europe, due to abundant chronicle sources and administrative records. Ming China is also well-documented through official court records. However, sources for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent. Portuguese exploration narratives are shaped by later nationalist historiography and may exaggerate the significance of early voyages. The historical record for 1433 is heavily weighted toward Europe and East Asia.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet: fifth major voyage underway
events.description: Zheng He's treasure fleet is in the midst of its fifth major voyage (1431–1433), which is exploring the Indian Ocean. The fleet, comprising hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel, is reaching East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian subcontinent. The voyage demonstrates Ming China's technological superiority in naval construction and navigation. However, the expeditions are increasingly opposed by Confucian officials at court, who view them as wasteful. The fifth voyage will be followed by a sixth voyage (1433–1435) and a seventh and final voyage (1433–1434), after which the Ming court will cease to fund large-scale maritime expeditions.

events.title: Italian Renaissance: Brunelleschi's dome of Florence Cathedral nears completion
events.description: Filippo Brunelleschi's dome of the Florence Cathedral (Duomo), one of the greatest architectural achievements of the Renaissance, is nearing completion in 1432. The dome, begun in 1420, represents a revolutionary engineering feat: it is the first large dome constructed without a wooden framework (centering), using instead an innovative double-shell design and herringbone brick pattern. The dome's completion (formally finished in 1436) marks a watershed moment in Renaissance architecture and engineering. Brunelleschi's achievement demonstrates the power of mathematical and engineering innovation and becomes a symbol of Florence's cultural and economic dominance. The dome's construction is documented in contemporary sources and represents one of the most significant technological achievements of the early 15th century.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (patron, though exiled in 1433)

sources.name: Antonio Manetti, Life of Brunelleschi (written in the late 15th century, based on contemporary accounts)

events.title: Ottoman Empire: consolidation of power in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Murad II (r. 1421–1444, 1446–1451), continues to consolidate power in Anatolia and the Balkans in 1432. The Ottomans have recently defeated the Venetian-Hungarian alliance in the Russo-Turkish War (1422–1423) and are expanding their control over the Balkans. In 1432, the Ottomans are at a period of relative stability, consolidating administrative control and preparing for further expansion. The empire is becoming the dominant military and political power in the eastern Mediterranean and the Balkans, a position that will be solidified under Murad II's successors, particularly Mehmed II (r. 1444–1446, 1451–1481), who will conquer Constantinople in 1453.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles and administrative records (fragmentary)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman history for this period is documented in Ottoman chronicles and contemporary Byzantine and Venetian sources, though Ottoman records are less detailed than European sources. The general context of Ottoman consolidation is confirmed, but specific events within 1432 are not precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Brunelleschi's dome as a revolutionary achievement is not disputed by modern scholarship, but some historians argue that the dome's engineering innovations have been exaggerated and that earlier domes (such as the Pantheon) employed similar principles. The claim that the dome was constructed without centering is technically accurate but somewhat misleading; temporary wooden supports were used during construction, though not a full framework. The Ottoman Empire's consolidation in 1432 is sometimes presented as inevitable, but modern scholarship emphasizes the contingency of Ottoman expansion and the role of internal factional conflicts and external pressures.
historiographic_note: The year 1432 is well-documented for Europe, particularly Italy and Central Europe, due to abundant chronicle sources and administrative records. Ming China is also well-documented through official court records. Ottoman sources are less detailed but provide some documentation. However, sources for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent. The historical record for 1432 is heavily weighted toward Europe and East Asia.

events.title: Joan of Arc executed; end of her military campaigns
events.description: Joan of Arc, the French peasant girl who claimed divine inspiration and led French military campaigns during the Hundred Years' War, is executed by burning in Rouen on 30 May 1431. Joan had been captured by Burgundian forces in 1430 and handed over to the English. She was tried for heresy and witchcraft by an ecclesiastical court dominated by English interests. Her execution marks the end of her military campaigns, which had begun in 1429 and had achieved significant victories, including the relief of Orléans and the coronation of Charles VII at Reims. Joan's death does not end the Hundred Years' War, but it marks a symbolic turning point: the French, inspired by Joan's legacy, continue to fight and eventually expel the English from France by 1453. Joan's execution is one of the most famous events of the medieval period and has become a symbol of French nationalism and religious martyrdom.
key_figures: Joan of Arc (French military leader, executed)
Charles VII (King of France, beneficiary of Joan's campaigns)
Henry VI (King of England, nominal beneficiary of Joan's capture)

events.title: Council of Constance: final sessions and ecclesiastical reform
events.description: The Council of Constance, which began in 1414, continues its final sessions in 1431. The council has already condemned Jan Hus (1415) and Jerome of Prague (1416) for heresy and has worked to heal the Western Schism. In 1431, the council is addressing remaining ecclesiastical reform issues and preparing for its formal conclusion in 1434. The council's work represents a major reassertion of papal authority and an attempt to address corruption and schism within the Catholic Church.

events.title: Council of Constance convenes Council of Siena; ecclesiastical continuity
events.description: As the Council of Constance prepares to conclude, ecclesiastical authorities convene the Council of Siena (1431–1449) to continue the work of ecclesiastical reform and to address ongoing religious controversies. The Council of Siena is called to address the Hussite question and to continue the reform agenda begun at Constance. The council represents the continuity of ecclesiastical authority and the determination of the Catholic Church to address heresy and reform. However, the Council of Siena is less significant than the Council of Constance and is often overshadowed by it in historical narratives.
key_figures: Pope Eugenius IV (successor to Martin V, reigning 1431–1447)

disconfirming_evidence: Joan of Arc's trial records, while extensive, are not entirely reliable: they were compiled by English-dominated ecclesiastical authorities with a vested interest in her conviction. Modern scholarship has questioned some details of her trial, particularly regarding the charges of witchcraft and the extent to which she was coerced into confessing. Some historians argue that Joan's military significance has been exaggerated by later French nationalist historiography; while her campaigns were important, they were not decisive in the Hundred Years' War, which was ultimately decided by broader political and military factors. The Council of Constance's significance in healing the Western Schism is sometimes overstated; the schism had already begun to heal before the council convened, and the council's role was more to formalize and consolidate existing trends. The Council of Siena is often overlooked in historical narratives, but it played an important role in continuing ecclesiastical reform.
historiographic_note: The year 1431 is well-documented for Europe, particularly France and Central Europe, due to abundant chronicle sources and administrative records. Joan of Arc's trial records are among the most detailed documents of the medieval period. Ming China is also well-documented through official court records. However, sources for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent. The historical record for 1431 is heavily weighted toward Europe and East Asia, with Joan of Arc's execution dominating Western historical narratives.

events.title: Joan of Arc captured by Burgundian forces; beginning of her captivity
events.description: Joan of Arc, the French peasant girl who claimed divine inspiration and led French military campaigns during the Hundred Years' War, is captured by Burgundian forces at Compiègne on 23 May 1430. Joan had been leading French military campaigns since 1429, achieving significant victories, including the relief of Orléans and the coronation of Charles VII at Reims. Her capture marks a turning point: she is handed over to the English, who arrange for her trial for heresy and witchcraft. Joan's captivity lasts approximately one year; she is executed by burning in Rouen on 30 May 1431. Her capture and subsequent execution become defining events of the Hundred Years' War and have profound symbolic significance for French nationalism and religious martyrdom.

events.title: Italian Renaissance: Donatello's sculptural innovations in Florence
events.description: Donatello (Donato di Niccolò di Betto Bardi), one of the greatest sculptors of the Renaissance, is active in Florence in 1430. Donatello is revolutionizing sculpture by introducing classical principles of proportion, anatomy, and perspective. In 1430, Donatello is working on major commissions, including sculptures for the Florence Cathedral and the Basilica of Santa Croce. His work represents a break from medieval sculptural traditions and a return to classical principles. Donatello's innovations in sculpture parallel Brunelleschi's innovations in architecture and Masaccio's innovations in painting, making Florence the center of Renaissance artistic innovation. Donatello's work will influence sculptors throughout Europe for centuries.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (patron, though exiled in 1433)

disconfirming_evidence: Joan of Arc's capture is sometimes presented as a sudden reversal of fortune, but modern scholarship emphasizes that her military campaigns had already begun to face setbacks before her capture. The claim that she was betrayed by Charles VII is disputed; Charles was unable to ransom her due to political and financial constraints, not because he abandoned her. The significance of Joan's capture and execution in determining the outcome of the Hundred Years' War is sometimes exaggerated; the war continued for another 23 years after her death, and the French victory was ultimately determined by broader political and military factors. The Council of Constance's work in 1430 is sometimes presented as nearly complete, but the council continued to address significant issues until its formal conclusion in 1434.
historiographic_note: The year 1430 is well-documented for Europe, particularly France and Central Europe, due to abundant chronicle sources and administrative records. Joan of Arc's capture is one of the most famous events of the medieval period and is extensively documented. Ming China is also well-documented through official court records. However, sources for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent. The historical record for 1430 is heavily weighted toward Europe and East Asia.

events.title: Joan of Arc captured at Compiègne
events.description: Joan of Arc, the French military leader who had rallied French forces and reversed English gains in the Hundred Years' War, was captured by Burgundian forces at Compiègne on 23 May 1429 during a sortie. She was subsequently sold to the English. Her capture marked a turning point in her legend and the broader conflict, though French momentum continued. Her imprisonment and eventual trial would dominate 1430–1431.
key_figures: Henry VI (English king, nominal claimant to French throne)

events.title: Zheng He's seventh and final voyage departs from China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He departed on his seventh and final treasure voyage from Nanjing in early 1429, commanding a massive fleet of junks. The voyage lasted until 1433 and reached the East African coast, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian Ocean rim. This expedition represented the apex of Ming maritime exploration and demonstrated Chinese naval and diplomatic power. After Zheng He's death in 1433, the Ming court would curtail such voyages, marking a shift toward continental focus.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming emperor, deceased 1424; voyage under successor Hongwu)

events.title: Siege of Orléans relieved; French military momentum restored
events.description: Following Joan of Arc's arrival in early 1429, French forces under her leadership relieved the English siege of Orléans in May 1429. This victory, achieved after months of English pressure, marked a decisive reversal in the Hundred Years' War. The relief of Orléans became a symbolic turning point and boosted French morale and recruitment. English forces, previously dominant in northern France, began a gradual retreat that would continue for decades.
key_figures: Jean de Dunois (French commander, 'the Bastard of Orléans')

events.title: Montezuma I crowned Aztec emperor
events.description: Montezuma I (Montezuma Ilhuicamina) was crowned as the eighth Aztec emperor in 1429, following the death of his predecessor. His reign (1429–1469) marked the beginning of the Aztec Empire's greatest territorial expansion and cultural flourishing. Montezuma I initiated military campaigns that extended Aztec dominion across Mesoamerica, reformed the tribute system, and commissioned major architectural and religious projects in Tenochtitlan. His reign is considered the apex of Aztec power before Spanish contact.
key_figures: Tlacaelel (Aztec high priest and advisor, influential throughout reign)

Aztec chronology for this period relies on indigenous records interpreted through Spanish colonial lenses; dating may have ±1–2 year uncertainty. The Codex Mendoza was created decades after the conquest under Spanish supervision.

events.title: Hussite Wars continue; Sigismund of Hungary campaigns in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars, which began in 1419 following the execution of Jan Hus, continued in 1429 with renewed military campaigns by Sigismund of Hungary (also Holy Roman Emperor) against the Hussite forces in Bohemia. The Hussites, a religious reform movement, had proven militarily formidable and resisted Catholic and imperial forces. The conflict remained unresolved, with neither side achieving decisive victory. The wars would continue until 1434 with the Battle of Lipany.
key_figures: Jan Žižka (Hussite military commander, deceased 1424; legacy continued)

events.title: Filippo Brunelleschi completes the dome of Florence Cathedral
events.description: Filippo Brunelleschi completed the construction of the dome (cupola) of Florence Cathedral (the Duomo) in 1429, a feat of engineering and architectural innovation. The dome, begun in 1420, was constructed without internal scaffolding using a novel double-shell technique and herringbone brick pattern. Its completion was celebrated as a triumph of Renaissance engineering and became an iconic symbol of Florence's cultural and economic power. The dome's success established Brunelleschi's reputation and influenced Renaissance architecture across Italy.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of African coast continues under Henry the Navigator
events.description: Portuguese maritime exploration of the West African coast, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continued in 1429. Portuguese caravels were pushing further south along the African coast, establishing trading posts and gathering knowledge of African geography, peoples, and resources. This period marked the beginning of the Portuguese Atlantic slave trade, though the scale was still limited. The exploration was motivated by desires for gold, spices, and Christian conversion, and would accelerate over the following decades.

disconfirming_evidence: Joan of Arc's military significance has been subject to modern historiographic revision. While her symbolic and morale impact is undisputed, some scholars argue that the relief of Orléans was primarily achieved by French commanders like Jean de Dunois and that Joan's tactical military contribution has been exaggerated in legend. Additionally, the notion that her capture in May 1429 immediately ended French momentum is complicated by the fact that French forces continued to gain ground militarily throughout 1429–1430 despite her absence. The completion date of Brunelleschi's dome is sometimes given as 1436 (consecration) rather than 1429 (structural completion); sources vary on whether 'completion' refers to structural finish or ceremonial dedication. Montezuma I's coronation date of 1429 relies entirely on post-conquest indigenous codices interpreted through Spanish colonial frameworks; no contemporary Aztec written records survive, and the Codex Mendoza was created under Spanish supervision in the 1540s, introducing potential bias and error.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1429 is heavily weighted toward European and East Asian sources. Joan of Arc's campaigns are among the most thoroughly documented events of the medieval period, with abundant English, French, and Burgundian records. Chinese sources for Zheng He's voyages are detailed and contemporary. However, African, Oceanic, and indigenous American histories are documented primarily through later European colonial accounts or oral traditions recorded centuries after the fact. The Aztec chronology depends entirely on post-conquest indigenous codices, which may have been altered or misinterpreted by Spanish friars. Sub-Saharan African history for this period is largely absent from surviving written records in European archives. The historical record is thus skewed toward literate, centralized states with written administrative traditions (Europe, China, Aztec Empire) and away from oral cultures, decentralized societies, and regions without extensive written archives.

events.title: Joan of Arc arrives at the French court in Chinon
events.description: Joan of Arc, a peasant girl from Domrémy claiming divine inspiration, arrived at the French court in Chinon in early 1429 (some sources date this to late 1428). She convinced the Dauphin Charles (later Charles VII) of her divine mission to save France from English domination. Her arrival marked a turning point in French military morale and strategy. Joan was subsequently given command of French forces and would lead the relief of Orléans in May 1429, reversing English gains in the Hundred Years' War.
key_figures: Charles VII (Dauphin of France, later king)
Jean de Metz (French knight who escorted Joan)

sources.name: Jean de Metz, contemporary account
Chronique de la Pucelle
French court records

events.certainty_note: Joan's arrival at Chinon is dated to early 1429 in most sources, though some chronicles place it in late 1428. The exact date is uncertain by ±1–2 months. Contemporary accounts confirm the event but vary slightly on timing.

events.title: English siege of Orléans begins
events.description: English forces under William de la Pole began a siege of the city of Orléans in October 1428, seeking to consolidate English control over northern France and cut off French supply lines. The siege lasted until May 1429, when Joan of Arc led French forces to relieve the city. The siege of Orléans became one of the most famous episodes of the Hundred Years' War and marked the beginning of English military decline in France.
key_figures: Jean de Dunois (French commander, 'the Bastard of Orléans')

sources.name: English military records and dispatches
Chronique de la Pucelle
French court records

events.certainty_note: The siege of Orléans is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the Hundred Years' War. The October 1428 start date is well-attested in English and French sources.

events.title: Zheng He's sixth voyage returns to China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's sixth treasure voyage returned to China in 1428 after departing in 1421. The voyage had reached the East African coast, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian Ocean rim, demonstrating Ming naval power and establishing diplomatic relations with numerous Indian Ocean states. The return of the sixth voyage marked the continuation of Ming maritime exploration, which would culminate in Zheng He's seventh and final voyage departing in 1429.

sources.name: Ming court records
Ma Huan, Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores

events.certainty_note: The return date of Zheng He's sixth voyage is well-documented in Ming archives. The voyage dates (1421–1428) are established in official records.

events.title: Cosimo de' Medici returns to Florence from exile
events.description: Cosimo de' Medici, the patriarch of the Medici banking family, returned to Florence in 1428 after a year of exile (1427–1428). His exile had been imposed by the rival Albizzi family, who feared Medici power. Cosimo's return marked the beginning of Medici political dominance in Florence, which would last for centuries. His return was facilitated by popular support and the weakening of the Albizzi faction. Cosimo would become the de facto ruler of Florence and a major patron of Renaissance art and learning.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (Florentine banker and political leader)
Rinaldo degli Albizzi (rival Florentine faction leader)

sources.name: Vespasiano da Bisticci, Lives of Illustrious Men (15th-century biography)
Florence city records and guild documents
Machiavelli, Florentine Histories (16th-century compilation)

events.certainty_note: Cosimo's return to Florence in 1428 is well-documented in Florentine city records and contemporary chronicles. The date is firmly established.

events.title: Tenochtitlan and Texcoco form the Triple Alliance
events.region: Tenochtitlan and Texcoco, Aztec Empire (modern Mexico)
events.description: The city-states of Tenochtitlan and Texcoco, along with Tlacopan, formalized a political and military alliance known as the Triple Alliance (Excan Tlatoloyan) in 1428. This alliance, which had been developing over the previous years, became the foundation of Aztec imperial power. The alliance allowed the three cities to coordinate military campaigns, share tribute, and expand their collective dominion across Mesoamerica. The Triple Alliance would remain the basis of Aztec political organization until the Spanish conquest in 1521.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza
Sahagún, Florentine Codex
Aztec oral tradition, recorded by Spanish chroniclers

events.certainty_note: The formation of the Triple Alliance is dated to 1428 in post-conquest Aztec codices, but the exact date may have ±2–3 year uncertainty. Some sources place the formalization slightly earlier or later. The alliance developed gradually rather than being formally declared on a single date.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Battle of Tachov
events.description: The Battle of Tachov was fought in 1428 between Hussite forces and Catholic/imperial forces during the Hussite Wars. The Hussites, a religious reform movement, achieved a military victory that further consolidated their control over Bohemia. The battle demonstrated the military effectiveness of Hussite tactics, particularly their use of wagon fortifications (Wagenburg) and early gunpowder weapons. The Hussite Wars would continue until 1434.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars
German and Hungarian military records

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Tachov in 1428 is well-documented in contemporary Bohemian and German sources.

events.title: Filippo Brunelleschi continues dome construction at Florence Cathedral
events.description: Filippo Brunelleschi continued construction of the dome of Florence Cathedral in 1428, refining his innovative double-shell technique and herringbone brick pattern. The dome was nearing completion and would be finished in 1429. Brunelleschi's engineering innovations, including the use of a wooden framework and the absence of internal scaffolding, were revolutionary for the time and demonstrated the technical sophistication of Renaissance engineering.

sources.name: Florence Cathedral building records
Vasari, Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: The progress of dome construction in 1428 is documented in Florence Cathedral's building records.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Joan of Arc's arrival at the French court is disputed. Some sources place it in late 1428, others in early 1429. The distinction is important for understanding the timeline of her rise to prominence. The formation of the Aztec Triple Alliance is traditionally dated to 1428, but modern scholarship suggests the alliance developed gradually over several years and may not have been formally constituted on a single date. Some scholars argue that the alliance was more of a military convenience than a formal political structure. Cosimo de' Medici's return to Florence in 1428 is well-documented, but the extent of his immediate political power is debated; some sources suggest his dominance developed gradually over the following years rather than being immediate upon his return.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1428 is heavily weighted toward European sources, particularly regarding Joan of Arc and the Hundred Years' War. Chinese sources for Zheng He's voyages are detailed and contemporary. However, Aztec history relies entirely on post-conquest indigenous codices interpreted through Spanish colonial frameworks. The Hussite Wars are well-documented in German, Bohemian, and Hungarian sources. African, Oceanic, and other non-literate societies are largely absent from surviving written records. The historical record is thus skewed toward literate, centralized states with written administrative traditions.

events.title: Death of the Yongle Emperor; accession of the Hongwu Emperor's grandson
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424) died in this year, marking the end of one of Ming China's most expansionist and culturally productive reigns. He was succeeded by his son, the Hongwu Emperor's grandson, whose reign would be shorter and less militarily aggressive. The transition consolidated the shift away from the aggressive foreign policy and massive treasure voyages that had defined Yongle's era. Court factionalism between eunuchs and scholar-officials intensified during the succession crisis.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Historiographic consensus from Ming studies

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's death date is well-documented in Ming court records and later compilations. The succession is confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Zheng He's seventh and final treasure voyage departs
events.description: Admiral Zheng He departed on his seventh and final treasure voyage from Nanjing in 1424 (some sources date the departure to 1433). This massive fleet of over 300 ships carried tens of thousands of crew and soldiers, visiting ports across the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Arabian Peninsula. The voyages represented the peak of Ming maritime technology and diplomatic ambition, though they were increasingly viewed as economically wasteful by scholar-officials at court. After Yongle's death, support for these expeditions waned, and they were eventually discontinued.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Zheng He's stele inscriptions
Later compilations: Ma Huan's Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The exact departure date of the seventh voyage is disputed. Some sources place it in 1424, others in 1433. The voyage itself is confirmed by multiple sources, but dating within this period is approximate.

events.title: Hussite Wars continue in Bohemia; Jan Žižka's campaigns
events.description: The Hussite Wars, sparked by the execution of Jan Hus in 1415, continued to rage across Bohemia in 1424. Jan Žižka, the blind Hussite military commander, led campaigns against Catholic forces and rival Hussite factions. Žižka's tactical innovations—particularly the use of wagon fortifications (Wagenburg)—made Hussite armies formidable. In 1424, Žižka was engaged in consolidating Hussite control over Bohemia and defending against external Catholic crusades. His death in October 1424 marked a turning point in the conflict.
key_figures: Sigismund of Hungary (Holy Roman Emperor, leading Catholic opposition)

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová (Hussite chronicler)
Historiographic consensus on Hussite Wars

events.certainty_note: Jan Žižka's death in October 1424 is well-documented in contemporary Hussite chronicles. The broader military campaigns are confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Montezuma I becomes Aztec Emperor
events.description: Montezuma I (Montezuma Ilhuicamina) ascended to the Aztec throne in 1424, following the death of his predecessor. His reign (1428–1467, though some sources date his accession to 1428) marked the beginning of the Aztec Empire's greatest territorial expansion. Montezuma I initiated military campaigns that extended Aztec tributary control across Mesoamerica, reformed the tribute system, and expanded the capital Tenochtitlan. He is remembered as one of the greatest Aztec rulers, though dating of his early reign is uncertain due to reliance on later Nahua sources and Spanish colonial accounts.
key_figures: Montezuma I (Montezuma Ilhuicamina, r. 1428–1467, accession disputed)

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (Aztec pictorial manuscript, compiled post-conquest)

Nahua oral traditions recorded by Spanish friars

events.certainty_note: Aztec chronology for this period relies heavily on later Nahua sources recorded by Spanish friars after the conquest (1521). The exact date of Montezuma I's accession is disputed; some sources place it in 1424, others in 1428. The year 1424 is assigned by convention in some scholarly works, but ±4 years is reasonable uncertainty.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates control in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: In 1424, the Ottoman Empire under Murad II (r. 1421–1444, 1446–1451) was consolidating its power in Anatolia and expanding into the Balkans. The Ottomans had recently defeated the Venetian-Hungarian alliance in the First Ottoman-Venetian War (1423–1430), securing their position in the eastern Mediterranean. Murad II's reign saw the establishment of Ottoman administrative structures and the integration of conquered territories. The empire was on the cusp of becoming a major Mediterranean and European power.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Neşri, Ashikpashazade)
Historiographic consensus on Ottoman expansion

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in this period is well-documented in Ottoman chronicles and European sources. The broader political consolidation is confirmed by multiple sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Montezuma I's accession is disputed in modern scholarship. While some sources place it in 1424, others argue for 1428 or even later. The Aztec chronology for this period is reconstructed from post-conquest Nahua sources and Spanish colonial accounts, which may have been influenced by Spanish chronological frameworks. The reliability of Aztec dates before the Spanish conquest (1521) is inherently limited. Additionally, some scholars question the significance attributed to Montezuma I's early reign, noting that the major territorial expansion occurred later in his rule (1430s–1460s). The dating of Zheng He's seventh voyage is also contested; some sources place the departure in 1433 rather than 1424, which would affect the significance of this year in the context of Ming maritime history.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1424 is heavily skewed toward East Asia (China) and Europe (Bohemia, Ottoman Empire), with rich documentary sources. The Aztec Empire is known primarily through post-conquest Nahua sources and Spanish colonial accounts, which introduce significant chronological uncertainty and potential bias. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Pacific Islands, and interior Americas are virtually absent from surviving written records for this year. The sources that do exist are often court-sponsored chronicles (Ming Shilu, Ottoman chronicles) or later compilations (Codex Mendoza, Sahagún), which may emphasize political and military events over economic, social, or cultural developments. The perspective is heavily weighted toward elite male actors and state-level politics.
