Southeast Asia (sparse contemporary documentation)

events.title: Battle of Ankara: Timur Defeats the Ottoman Empire
events.description: On 28 July 1402, Timur (Tamerlane) decisively defeats the Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I at the Battle of Ankara, one of the most significant military engagements of the medieval world. Bayezid is captured and dies in captivity in 1403. The Ottoman Empire is temporarily fragmented, with rival claimants competing for power. This battle halts Ottoman expansion for over a decade and allows the Byzantine Empire a brief respite. However, the Ottomans eventually recover and resume their expansion, ultimately conquering Constantinople in 1453. The battle demonstrates the power of Timur's military machine and the vulnerability of even the rising Ottoman state.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi
Ottoman chronicles (Neşri, Ashikpashazade)
Byzantine sources (Doukas, Chalkokondyles)

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple independent sources from Islamic, Ottoman, and Byzantine traditions. The date of 28 July 1402 is well-established.

events.title: Yongle Emperor Consolidates Power; Jingnan Rebellion Ends
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di) consolidates his power after the successful conclusion of the Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402), in which he defeated his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor, and seized the throne. The rebellion ends with the death or disappearance of the Jianwen Emperor (the circumstances remain historically disputed). The Yongle Emperor establishes a new regime characterized by centralized authority, military expansion, and cultural patronage. He will rule until 1424 and is remembered as one of the greatest Ming emperors, responsible for the construction of the Forbidden City, the Yongle Encyclopedia, and the sponsorship of Zheng He's voyages.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Historical records compiled by later Ming historians

events.certainty_note: The end of the Jingnan Rebellion and the Yongle Emperor's consolidation of power are well-documented. However, the fate of the Jianwen Emperor remains disputed; some sources suggest he escaped and lived in hiding.

events.title: Henry IV of England Consolidates Power; Glyndŵr's Rebellion Continues
events.description: Henry IV of England (r. 1399–1413) continues to consolidate his power after deposing Richard II in 1399. In Wales, Owain Glyndŵr's rebellion (1400–1415) is ongoing, challenging English authority. By 1402, Henry IV is dealing with multiple threats: the Glyndŵr rebellion, baronial opposition, and religious dissent (Lollards). Despite these challenges, Henry is gradually establishing his authority and legitimacy. The year 1402 marks a period of relative stability compared to the chaos of the 1390s.

sources.name: Chronicles of Adam of Usk
Parliamentary records (Rotuli Parliamentorum)

events.certainty_note: Henry IV's consolidation of power and the Glyndŵr rebellion are well-documented in contemporary English sources.

events.title: Council of Pisa Preparations; Western Schism Continues
events.description: The Western Schism in the Catholic Church (1378–1417) continues, with multiple claimants to the papacy. Preparations are underway for the Council of Pisa (1409), which will attempt to resolve the schism by deposing both rival popes and electing a new one. By 1402, the schism has lasted over two decades, creating confusion and division within Christendom. The council will ultimately fail to resolve the schism immediately, but it represents an important effort at ecclesiastical reform and centralization.

sources.name: Papal registers and ecclesiastical records
Chronicles of church historians

events.certainty_note: The Western Schism and preparations for the Council of Pisa are well-documented in ecclesiastical records.

events.title: Venice Consolidates Maritime Power; Venetian-Ottoman Tensions Rise
events.description: Venice, the dominant maritime power in the Mediterranean, is consolidating its commercial and political influence. The Ottoman defeat at Ankara in 1402 temporarily reduces Ottoman pressure on Venetian interests in the eastern Mediterranean. Venice uses this period to strengthen its position in the Adriatic and eastern Mediterranean, securing trade routes and establishing diplomatic relationships with various powers. The Venetian Republic is at the height of its power and influence during this period.

sources.name: Venetian state archives (Archivio di Stato di Venezia)

events.certainty_note: Venetian diplomatic and commercial activity is well-documented in archives, though specific events in 1402 may be difficult to isolate.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration Advances; Early Voyages to African Coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, continue their systematic exploration of the African coast. By 1402, Portuguese expeditions have reached the Canary Islands and are beginning to explore the West African coast in earnest. These voyages are motivated by commercial interests (gold, spices, slaves) and religious zeal. The Portuguese are developing new maritime technologies and navigation techniques that will eventually enable the Age of Exploration and the circumnavigation of Africa.

sources.name: Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea by Gomes Eanes de Zurara

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration is attested in later chronicles; specific dates for individual voyages in 1402 are uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Ankara is sometimes dated to 20 July 1402 rather than 28 July; modern scholarship (e.g., Reuben Levy) generally accepts 28 July as the correct date. The fate of the Jianwen Emperor is disputed; some sources suggest he escaped and lived in hiding, while others claim he died in the rebellion. This uncertainty has led to historical debate about the legitimacy of the Yongle Emperor's rule. The significance of the Battle of Ankara is sometimes overstated; while it was a major victory for Timur, it did not permanently destroy the Ottoman Empire, which recovered and eventually became the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean. The Western Schism is sometimes portrayed as a crisis of faith, but it was primarily a political dispute over papal authority and legitimacy.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1402 is rich for Europe and China, with abundant administrative records, chronicles, and later historical compilations. Ottoman sources are less abundant but still available. Italian sources are particularly detailed regarding Venice and Florence. African, American, and Oceanic history remains almost entirely absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often produced by and for the ruling classes, potentially obscuring the experiences of common people, women, and non-literate societies. Chinese sources may be biased toward the Yongle Emperor's legitimacy, while Ottoman sources may downplay the significance of the Ankara defeat.

The Italian Renaissance is flourishing, with humanist scholars and artists reshaping European culture.

events.title: Jingnan Rebellion Continues; Yongle Emperor Advances
events.description: The Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402) continues, with the Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di) making significant military advances against his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor. By 1401, the Yongle Emperor has captured several key cities and is consolidating his military position. The rebellion is characterized by brutal warfare, with both sides employing large armies and engaging in siege warfare. The conflict will culminate in the Yongle Emperor's victory in 1402 and his consolidation of power. This rebellion is one of the most significant civil conflicts in Ming history and has profound implications for the dynasty's future.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Jingnan Rebellion is well-documented in Ming administrative records and later historical compilations.

events.title: Timur Prepares for Ottoman Campaign; Diplomatic Tensions Rise
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the great Central Asian conqueror, is preparing for his invasion of Anatolia and the Ottoman Empire. By 1401, tensions between Timur and the Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I are escalating. Timur is consolidating his forces and preparing for the campaign that will culminate in the Battle of Ankara in 1402. This period of preparation involves diplomatic negotiations, military mobilization, and strategic planning. The campaign will be one of Timur's last major military undertakings before his death in 1405.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi

events.certainty_note: Timur's preparations for the Ottoman campaign are attested in later chronicles; specific events in 1401 are less clearly documented.

events.title: Henry IV of England Faces Continued Baronial Opposition
events.description: Henry IV of England (r. 1399–1413) continues to face baronial opposition and internal instability. By 1401, Henry has been on the throne for two years and is working to consolidate his power and legitimacy. He faces threats from various baronial factions, including the Percy family, who will rebel in 1403. The year 1401 is characterized by political tension and the gradual consolidation of royal authority. Henry's reign is marked by constant vigilance against potential rivals and rebels.

sources.name: Chronicles of Adam of Usk

events.certainty_note: Henry IV's political situation is well-documented in contemporary English chronicles.

events.title: Owain Glyndŵr's Rebellion in Wales Escalates
events.description: Owain Glyndŵr's rebellion (1400–1415) against English rule in Wales is escalating in 1401. Glyndŵr, a Welsh nobleman, has declared himself Prince of Wales and is leading a nationalist uprising against English authority. By 1401, the rebellion has spread across much of Wales and is attracting support from various Welsh lords and common people. The rebellion will continue for over a decade and will pose a significant challenge to Henry IV's authority. Glyndŵr's rebellion is one of the last major Welsh uprisings against English rule.

sources.name: Chronicles of Adam of Usk
Welsh historical traditions

events.certainty_note: Glyndŵr's rebellion is attested in contemporary English chronicles and later Welsh sources.

events.title: Venice Maintains Maritime Dominance; Trade Routes Secured
events.description: Venice, the dominant maritime power in the Mediterranean, is maintaining and consolidating its commercial dominance. The Republic is securing trade routes, establishing trading posts, and engaging in diplomatic negotiations with various powers. Venice's wealth is based on its control of Mediterranean trade, particularly the spice trade from the East. By 1401, Venice is at the height of its power and influence, with a sophisticated commercial and financial system that makes it one of the wealthiest cities in Europe.

sources.name: Venetian state archives (Archivio di Stato di Venezia)

events.certainty_note: Venetian commercial activity is well-documented in archives, though specific events in 1401 may be difficult to isolate.

events.title: Florence and Milan Engage in Diplomatic Negotiations
events.description: Florence and Milan are engaged in complex diplomatic negotiations as part of the ongoing power struggle among Italian city-states. The two cities are competing for influence and territory, while also engaging in cultural and commercial exchange. By 1401, the balance of power in Italy is shifting, with Florence gradually gaining influence under the Medici family. These diplomatic activities are characteristic of the period, as Italian states compete for dominance while also contributing to the cultural flourishing of the Renaissance.

sources.name: Florentine state archives

events.certainty_note: Italian diplomatic activity is well-documented in state archives, though specific events in 1401 may vary by ±1 year.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact timeline of the Jingnan Rebellion is sometimes disputed; some sources give slightly different dates for key battles and events. The significance of Owain Glyndŵr's rebellion is sometimes overstated; while it was a major uprising, it did not fundamentally alter the balance of power between Wales and England. The rebellion is sometimes portrayed as a nationalist uprising, but it was also driven by personal grievances and feudal disputes. Timur's preparations for the Ottoman campaign are less well-documented than the campaign itself; the exact timing of diplomatic tensions is unclear.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1401 is moderate for Europe and China, with administrative records and chronicles available. Ottoman sources are less abundant. Italian sources are detailed regarding Florence and Venice. African, American, and Oceanic history remains almost entirely absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often produced by and for the ruling classes, potentially obscuring the experiences of common people, women, and non-literate societies. Chinese sources may be biased toward the Yongle Emperor's legitimacy, while English sources may downplay the significance of Glyndŵr's rebellion.
